summary: Spanish classical guitarist and composer (1852–1909), considered the father of modern classical guitar technique. Best known for Recuerdos de la Alhambra (a tremolo étude inspired by the Moorish palace in Granada) and Gran Vals (whose melody became the Nokia mobile phone ringtone). Also wrote Capricho Árabe, Danza Mora, and a series of preludes including Lagrima, Endecha, and Oremus. Transcribed works by Albeniz, Bach, and Mozart for guitar. Suffered a right-side paralysis late in his career. Student of Julián Arcas.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Francisco Tárrega (1852–1909) was a Spanish classical guitarist and composer, widely regarded as the father of modern classical guitar technique. He was a student of Julián Arcas. Late in his career he suffered a right-side paralysis that severely limited his playing. He transcribed many works by Isaac Albéniz (including Asturias/Leyenda), Bach, and Mozart for guitar. His playing technique influenced his successor Andrés Segovia, who however broke from Tárrega's tradition by playing with a combination of fingernails and fingertips. Wikipedia

works.name: Recuerdos de la Alhambra
works.description: Tárrega's most famous guitar piece (c. 1896), a tremolo étude inspired by the Moorish Alhambra palace in Granada. The i, m, and a fingers play the same melodic note in rapid succession (tremolo) while the thumb plays a counter-melody. Often paired with Capricho Árabe. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A tremolo étude for guitar; i, m, and a fingers play the melody tremolo while the thumb plays a counter-melody; inspired by the Moorish Alhambra palace in Granada
examples: This Spanish guitar virtuoso drew inspiration from the drums of Algeria to write his Danza Mora, but his single most famous piece is a solo work inspired by a Moorish palace, entitled Recuerdos de la Alhambra

clues.clue: The opening melody is an ornamented sequence: half-note E, quarter D, half C, quarter D, long E — then transposed up to start on G, then an answering phrase from high C landing on G-sharp

works.name: Gran Vals
works.description: A waltz for solo guitar by Tárrega, whose main theme became the Nokia mobile phone ringtone from the 1990s onward. The Nokia ringtone melody consists of eighth notes D–E, quarter F–G, then the same shifted down two semitones, then down one more, then a dotted half note on A. Wikipedia

clues.clue: The source of the Nokia mobile phone ringtone; a waltz for solo guitar
examples: If you had a Nokia phone from the 1990s onwards, your ringtone might be from Gran Vals, a waltz by this nineteenth-century Spanish guitar composer
The Nokia ringtone was excerpted from a piece for this instrument called Gran Vals written by Francisco Tarrega

works.name: Capricho Árabe
works.description: A guitar piece by Tárrega composed after travels through Andalusia and North Africa. Opens with a long D power chord played as a natural harmonic (a perfect fifth). Calls for extensive portamento. Often paired with Recuerdos de la Alhambra.

works.description: A short Moorish dance for guitar by Tárrega, written the morning after hearing a repetitive drum rhythm in Algiers.

comprehensive_summary:
Francisco Tárrega (1852–1909) was a Spanish classical guitarist and composer, considered the founder of modern classical guitar technique and a central figure in establishing the guitar as a serious concert instrument. He studied under Julián Arcas and became a celebrated virtuoso, though a right-side paralysis near the peak of his career curtailed his playing. He wrote the paired preludes Endecha and Oremus shortly before his death. He wrote 14 standard preludes for guitar in total, the three named ones being 'Lagrima' (after audience members at a concert in England reported feeling sad), 'Endecha,' and 'Oremus.' He transcribed a great many works by Albéniz (including Asturias/Leyenda), Bach, and Mozart for guitar, doing much to establish the classical guitar repertoire. Andrés Segovia, the next generation's defining guitarist, built on but also departed from Tárrega's tradition (playing with a mix of fingernails and fingertips rather than fingertips alone).

His most famous original work is Recuerdos de la Alhambra (c. 1896), a tremolo étude inspired by the Moorish Alhambra palace in Granada. In the piece, the i, m, and a fingers rapidly alternate on the same melodic note (tremolo) while the thumb plays an accompanying counter-melody. The opening melody is an ornamented version of the pitches E – D – C – D – E (then transposed up), with an answering phrase from high C landing on G-sharp. His Gran Vals, a waltz for solo guitar, became famous worldwide when its main theme was adopted as the Nokia mobile phone ringtone in the 1990s; the Nokia melody consists of D–E (eighth notes), F–G (quarters), then the same figure transposed down twice, ending on a dotted half note on A. His Capricho Árabe, written after travels through Andalusia and North Africa, opens with a long D perfect-fifth natural harmonic and calls for extensive portamento. His Danza Mora was composed the morning after hearing a repetitive drum rhythm in Algiers.

Étude: tremolo guitar piece inspired by the Moorish Alhambra palace in Granada; i, m, and a fingers play the melody tremolo while the thumb plays a counter-melody
Étude: guitar waltz whose main theme became the Nokia mobile phone ringtone
Étude: guitar piece composed after travels in Andalusia and North Africa; opens with a D perfect-fifth chord played as a natural harmonic; calls for extensive portamento
Étude: short Moorish dance for guitar written the morning after the composer heard a repetitive drum rhythm in Algiers
